title: Writing a Blog Post End-to-End: Agent Era Sustained Evolution
summary: Starting from a Cognition Ladder diagram drawn last year, to writing a blog post about crossing the L3→L4 chasm. The entire process — HTML diagrams, bilingual content, cover images, six-platform distribution — was done with Claude Code + ink-flow + image-x + OPC review. Not a tutorial, but a field recording of agent-era content creation.
body:
## The Trigger: A Diagram from Last Year

Last year I drew a diagram called the Cognition Ladder, categorizing our team's AI/Agent understanding into five levels: Awareness → Serious Reading → Demo → Product → Business.

At the time, we performed well at L1-L3 — lots of discussions, cutting-edge experiments, internal incubation. But L4 (production deployment), we rarely reached. Most attempts stopped at L3.

A year later, two things happened:

1. We launched Societas — Microsoft's first agent product. 99% AI-coded, open-source stack, industry-leading GAIA benchmark results. We genuinely crossed the L3-to-L4 chasm.
2. The industry shifted — Claude Code, OpenClaw, various agent tools extended capability boundaries from different angles.

I wanted to write a blog post documenting this crossing, and my understanding of "sustained evolution."

## The L3 Trap: 60% and 40%

One of the blog's core arguments:

> When building with LLMs/Agents, the first 60% comes fast — you nail a happy path and feel great about yourself. But the remaining 40% is where the real difficulty lies, and every time a major model upgrade happens, that 40% shifts.

Demos only need the happy path. Production users input things you never imagined. You think you've covered 90% of scenarios, then discover the remaining 10% is the real challenge.

That's the chasm between L3 and L4.

## HTML Diagrams: image-x Mode B

The blog needed structured diagrams — Cognition Ladder, capability matrix, evolution timeline. These aren't suited for AI generation (too structurally precise), but I didn't want to manually draw them in Figma either.

Solution: write diagrams in HTML, then use image-x skill's Mode B (html2png) for screenshots.

```
/image-x Mode B: screenshot this HTML file
```

Under the hood it's Playwright headless: open HTML → wait for networkidle → capture specified region → output PNG. Since it's HTML, I write one version per language, screenshot each.

The image-x skill itself went through OPC review. The review caught issues: DashScope API's size parameter was deprecated (`832*480` no longer works), GIF pipeline's ffmpeg parameters needed adjustment. After fixes, the skill became more robust.

## Bilingual Content: Rewriting, Not Translating

The Chinese and English versions aren't translations of each other. The Chinese version targets domestic readers who understand Microsoft's internal context; the English version targets global developers who need more background.

Conventions:
- Chinese: `agent-era-sustained-evolution.md`
- English: `agent-era-sustained-evolution--en.md` (double dash)
- Shared images: `public/images/{slug}/`, English-specific images get `-en.png` suffix

Technical terms stay in English (L3 Trap, happy path, edge case) — this is a rule in ink-flow's SKILL.md. English titles should be compelling, not literal — 「持续进化」 becomes "Sustained Evolution" (more tension) rather than "Continuous Evolution."

## Cover Image Generation

Cover image used image-x Mode A (AI generation):

```
/image-x A dark-themed abstract tech illustration expressing "cognition ladder" and "evolution"
```

DashScope's wan model generated candidates. Picked one with gradient colors and geometric shapes, resized to each platform's requirements (WeChat 900×383, Zhihu 1920×1080, LinkedIn 1200×627).

Each platform has different cover dimensions — this knowledge lives in `platforms/wechat/spec.py`.

## Six-Platform Distribution

After writing, ink-flow's distribute workflow pushed to six platforms:

1. **Blog (Chinese)** — `git push` triggers Vercel auto-deploy
2. **Blog (EN)** — same mechanism, English version
3. **WeChat Official Account** — HTML format, lint.py checks `<pre>` and `<style>` issues
4. **Zhihu** — HTML format, lint.py checks Zhihu-specific rules
5. **Twitter Thread** — markdown split into tweet chain
6. **LinkedIn Post** — single markdown post

preview.py's dashboard was used for final confirmation before distribution: every platform's preview looked correct, no formatting breakage.

## OPC Review of Blog Content

Before publishing, I ran `/opc` to review the blog content itself. Selected PM, new-user, and active-user roles.

Cross-signals were far more valuable than any single role's suggestions:
- PM felt the L3 Trap argument was too abstract
- new-user couldn't understand what Societas was
- active-user felt the ending was weak

All three roles flagged the ending — that's P0 priority. Fixed it.

## Looking Back

The entire process — from initial idea to six-platform publication — spanned multiple sessions, roughly 5-6 hours total. Without ink-flow + image-x + OPC review, the same work would take at least a full day, with more formatting errors.

But the biggest takeaway isn't efficiency. It's **the shift in creative experience.** I no longer worry about "is the WeChat formatting correct" or "will Zhihu strip the style tags" — those are L3-level problems. I focus on content: is the argument sound, is the story compelling.

That's agent-era content creation: hand mechanical work to the pipeline, keep creative work for yourself.
